metadata.name: Ukraine Neon Gas Supply Disruption
metadata.source: Techcet / SEMI / Reuters / US Department of Commerce
metadata.description: Russia's invasion of Ukraine disrupted roughly 50% of the global supply of semiconductor-grade neon gas, a critical input for lithography processes in chip fabrication. Ukraine's Mariupol and Odesa plants (Ingas, Cryoin) supplied the majority of the world's purified neon. Prices surged over 600% in the weeks following the invasion. While large fabs like TSMC and Samsung had 3-6 month strategic reserves, smaller fabs and those dependent on spot markets faced immediate constraints.

recovery_milestones.description: Russia invades Ukraine; Mariupol and Odesa neon plants halt production
Spot neon prices begin rapid escalation
Neon prices reach +600% over pre-war levels
Alternative sources (China, US) begin ramping; still insufficient
Major fabs begin qualifying alternative neon suppliers
Non-Ukraine neon capacity expansion projects underway globally
Supply broadly diversified; prices partially normalized but remain elevated

lessons_learned: Critical semiconductor inputs like neon gas had extreme geographic concentration in a conflict zone, yet this was widely underappreciated until the crisis struck.
Strategic reserves at major fabs (TSMC, Samsung: 3-6 months) bought crucial time, validating buffer inventory strategies for critical materials.
The neon supply chain had a hidden Russia dependency: Ukraine purified neon, but much of the raw feedstock originated from Russian steel mills.
Post-crisis diversification to Chinese and US neon sources reduced Ukraine dependence from 50% to under 25% within 18 months.
Cascading geopolitical risks (conflict, sanctions, logistics disruption) can simultaneously affect multiple critical material inputs (neon, palladium, nickel, wheat).
